fact_pattern: Ramesh agreed to supply 500 kg of wheat to Suresh by March 1st for Rs. 50,000. Ramesh failed to deliver. Suresh had to buy from another supplier at Rs. 65,000.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73 - Compensation for loss or damage caused by breach of contract

precedents.case_name: Satyabrata Ghose v Mugneeram Bangur (1954 SC)
precedents.rationale: Damages bounded by frustration of contract principles in Indian law.

precedents.case_name: Murlidhar Chiranjilal v Harishchandra Dwarkadas (1962)
precedents.rationale: Seller liable for market price difference upon breach.

gold_label_reasoning: Clear breach of contract. Damages of Rs.15,000 recoverable under Section 73.

fact_pattern: Priya hired a contractor to renovate her shop within 30 days for Rs. 2,00,000. The contractor completed the work in 45 days. Priya refused to pay claiming full breach.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 55 - Effect of failure to perform at fixed time

precedents.case_name: Startup v Macdonald (1843)
precedents.rationale: Substantial performance may still entitle contractor to payment minus damages for delay.

gold_label_reasoning: Contractor liable for delay damages but entitled to payment for completed work.

fact_pattern: Anil booked a wedding hall for Rs. 5,00,000 six months in advance. The hall owner cancelled 2 weeks before the wedding citing renovation needs. Anil had already paid Rs. 2,00,000 as advance.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73 - Compensation for loss or damage caused by breach of contract
Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 65 - Obligation of person who has received advantage under void agreement

precedents.case_name: Alopi Parshad v Union of India (1960)
precedents.rationale: Party cancelling contract must compensate for foreseeable losses caused.

gold_label_reasoning: Hall owner breached contract. Full advance refund plus consequential damages for alternative venue costs recoverable.

fact_pattern: Meera sold her car to Raj for Rs. 3,00,000. After payment, Meera discovered the car had an undisclosed lien from a bank loan. The bank repossessed the car from Raj.
applicable_statutes: Sale of Goods Act 1930, Section 14 - Implied condition as to title
Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 17 - Fraud

precedents.case_name: Rowland v Divall (1923)
precedents.rationale: Seller without clear title cannot pass title. Buyer entitled to full refund.

gold_label_reasoning: Seller had no clear title. Buyer entitled to full refund under Section 14 of Sale of Goods Act.

fact_pattern: Vikram agreed to paint Sunita's house for Rs. 80,000 in 10 days. He finished in 10 days but used a slightly different shade of white than specified. Sunita refused to pay the full amount.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 37 - Obligation of parties to contracts
Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 55 - Effect of failure to perform at fixed time

precedents.case_name: Hoenig v Isaacs (1952)
precedents.rationale: Substantial performance entitles contractor to payment minus cost of making good the defect.

gold_label_reasoning: Substantial performance achieved. Sunita must pay Rs. 80,000 minus small deduction for color correction cost.

fact_pattern: A hospital left a surgical sponge inside a patient during an operation. The patient suffered infection 3 months later. The hospital claims the infection was unrelated.
applicable_statutes: Law of Torts - Negligence
Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2(42) - Deficiency in service

precedents.case_name: Jacob Mathew v State of Punjab (2005)
precedents.rationale: Res ipsa loquitur applies in medical negligence â€” the thing speaks for itself.

precedents.case_name: Spring Meadows Hospital v Harjol Ahluwalia (1998)
precedents.rationale: Hospital vicariously liable for staff negligence during treatment.

gold_label_reasoning: Res ipsa loquitur â€” surgical sponge inside patient speaks for itself. Hospital is liable.

fact_pattern: A pedestrian jaywalked and was hit by a car travelling 10 km/h over the speed limit. Both parties claim the other is at fault.
applicable_statutes: Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Section 166 - Application for compensation
Law of Torts - Contributory Negligence

precedents.case_name: Minu B. Mehta v Balkrishna (1977)
precedents.rationale: Contributory negligence reduces but does not eliminate driver's liability.

gold_label_reasoning: Contributory negligence of pedestrian is primary cause. Driver liability significantly reduced. Verdict: not liable for full damages.

fact_pattern: A school bus driver ran a red light and collided with a motorcycle. The motorcyclist was not wearing a helmet as required by law. The motorcyclist suffered head injuries.
applicable_statutes: Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Section 129 - Wearing of protective headgear
Law of Torts - Contributory Negligence
Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Section 166 - Application for compensation

precedents.case_name: Pratap Narain Singh Deo v Srinivas Sabata (1976)
precedents.rationale: Driver running red light is primarily negligent. Victim's contributory negligence reduces but does not eliminate liability.

gold_label_reasoning: Driver primarily liable for red light violation. Damages reduced by 25% for contributory negligence due to no helmet.

fact_pattern: A restaurant served peanut-containing food to a customer who had clearly informed the waiter of a severe peanut allergy. The customer suffered anaphylactic shock and was hospitalized.
applicable_statutes: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2(9) - Consumer rights
Law of Torts - Negligence
Food Safety and Standards Act 2006 - Labelling requirements

precedents.case_name: M.C. Mehta v Union of India (1987 SC)
precedents.rationale: Strict liability applies for inherent negligence causing harm.

precedents.case_name: Peppiat v Rooney (1954)
precedents.rationale: Restaurant liable when customer clearly discloses allergy and staff ignores the warning.

gold_label_reasoning: Clear duty of care breached after explicit allergy disclosure. Restaurant fully liable for medical damages.

fact_pattern: Grandfather died without a will. Three grandchildren claim inheritance. Child A has a registered sale deed from grandfather dated 2018. Child B claims the deed was signed under coercion. Child C claims adverse possession for 15 years.
applicable_statutes: Hindu Succession Act 1956, Section 8 - General rules of succession
Transfer of Property Act 1882, Section 53A - Part performance
Limitation Act 1963, Section 65 - Adverse possession

precedents.case_name: Ravinder Kaur Grewal v Manjit Kaur (2019)
precedents.rationale: 12 years of continuous possession can override registered title under Limitation Act.

precedents.case_name: Vidhyadhar v Manikrao (1999)
precedents.rationale: Registered sale deed presumed valid unless fraud proven by clear and cogent evidence.

gold_label_reasoning: Registered sale deed (Child A) presumed valid. Coercion unsubstantiated. Adverse possession claim insufficient without proof of continuous, hostile possession.

fact_pattern: Two brothers inherited a shop equally from their father. Brother A ran the shop for 20 years and made improvements worth Rs. 10 lakhs. Brother B now claims 50% of the improved value. Brother A claims full ownership by adverse possession.
applicable_statutes: Hindu Succession Act 1956, Section 19 - Mode of succession of two or more heirs
Limitation Act 1963, Section 65 - Adverse possession of immovable property
Transfer of Property Act 1882, Section 44 - Transfer by one co-owner

precedents.case_name: Keshav v Narayan (2001)
precedents.rationale: Co-owner cannot claim adverse possession against another co-owner during joint ownership.

precedents.case_name: Joginder Kumar v State of UP (1994)
precedents.rationale: Improvements by one co-owner entitle them to reimbursement before partition.

gold_label_reasoning: Co-owner cannot claim adverse possession. Brother B entitled to 50% but Brother A entitled to reimbursement for improvement costs before partition.

fact_pattern: A tenant has lived in a property for 25 years under a rent agreement. The landlord died and the heir wants to evict the tenant claiming the rent agreement expired. The tenant has receipts for rent paid continuously.
applicable_statutes: Transfer of Property Act 1882, Section 106 - Duration of certain leases
Rent Control Act - Statutory tenant protection
Limitation Act 1963 - Relevant limitation periods

precedents.case_name: Atma Ram Properties v Federal Motors (2005)
precedents.rationale: Long-term tenant with continuous rent payment record has statutory protected tenancy rights.

gold_label_reasoning: 25 years of continuous tenancy with rent receipts creates statutory protected tenancy. Eviction not valid without proper notice under Rent Control Act.

fact_pattern: A developer constructed an extra floor on a building without written permission. The municipality ordered demolition. The developer claims the extra floor was approved verbally by a municipal officer. Residents below fear structural damage.
applicable_statutes: Municipal Corporation Act - Mandatory written building approval
Environment Protection Act 1986 - Construction standards
Transfer of Property Act 1882 - Rights of property owners

precedents.case_name: Dipak Kumar v Municipal Corporation (2010)
precedents.rationale: Verbal permissions have no legal standing for construction. Written approval is mandatory under municipal law.

gold_label_reasoning: No written permit exists. Verbal approval has no legal standing. Municipality's demolition order is valid.

fact_pattern: A woman claims ownership of land through a will made by her uncle. Her cousin contests the will claiming the uncle was mentally unsound when signing. Two doctors give conflicting testimonies about the uncle's mental state at the time of signing.
applicable_statutes: Indian Succession Act 1925, Section 59 - Testamentary capacity
Indian Evidence Act 1872, Section 45 - Opinions of experts
Hindu Succession Act 1956 - Intestate succession fallback

precedents.case_name: Smt. Bhagwati v Bharat Singh (1966)
precedents.rationale: Will propounder must prove testamentary capacity when genuinely and seriously contested.

precedents.case_name: H. Venkatachala v B.N. Thimmajamma (1959)
precedents.rationale: Registered will carries strong presumption of validity. Burden of proof lies on the contestant.

gold_label_reasoning: Registered will carries presumption of validity. Conflicting testimony alone is insufficient to overturn. Woman's claim upheld.

fact_pattern: Traffic collision involving a 17-year-old minor driving a vehicle, resulting in property damage. The minor fled the scene. Video evidence and license plate submitted by victim.
applicable_statutes: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 125 - Act endangering life or personal safety of others
Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Section 199A - Offences by Juveniles

precedents.case_name: State vs. Juvenile Driver (2025)
precedents.rationale: Parents of minor held liable for allowing unauthorized driving. Minor sent to Juvenile Justice Board.

fact_pattern: An applicant filed an RTI request for details on local road construction funds. The Public Information Officer (PIO) denied the request citing 'national security' without providing a specific exemption clause. The applicant is appealing to the Information Commission.
applicable_statutes: Right to Information Act 2005, Section 8 - Exemption from disclosure of information
Right to Information Act 2005, Section 19 - Appeals

precedents.case_name: Central Board of Secondary Education vs. Aditya Bandopadhyay (2011)
precedents.rationale: Public authorities must justify exemptions specifically; general claims of security are insufficient.

gold_label_reasoning: RTI denial was invalid as road funds do not fall under Section 8 security exemptions. PIO directed to release data.

fact_pattern: A local restaurant's health license was cancelled after a single inspection report found minor hygiene issues. The owner claims the penalty is disproportionate and was done without a proper show-cause notice.
applicable_statutes: FSSAI Act 2006 - Licensing and Registration
Principles of Natural Justice - Audi Alteram Partem (Hear the other side)

precedents.case_name: Maneka Gandhi vs. Union of India (1978)
precedents.rationale: Administrative action affecting rights must follow fair procedure and natural justice.

gold_label_reasoning: Cancellation without show-cause notice violates natural justice. License must be reinstated pending a fair hearing.

fact_pattern: An employee was terminated without notice after an interpersonal dispute with the manager that was unrelated to work performance.
applicable_statutes: Industrial Disputes Act 1947, Section 2A - Dismissal of an individual workman
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 351 - Criminal intimidation (if applicable)

precedents.case_name: Chandigarh Administration vs. Jagjit Singh (1995)
precedents.rationale: Termination without valid reason or due process is arbitrary and illegal.

gold_label_reasoning: Termination for personal reasons without following due process violates labor rights and principles of natural justice.

fact_pattern: Incident 15: The accused threatened the victim in a public space, brandishing a weapon, demanding money.

precedents.case_name: State of Maharashtra v Madhurkar Narayan
precedents.rationale: Clear mens rea.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

fact_pattern: Incident 16: Supplier failed to deliver goods due to sudden market price spike, claiming force majeure.

precedents.case_name: Satyabrata Ghose v Mugneeram Bangur (1954 SC)
precedents.rationale: Price rise is not force majeure.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

fact_pattern: Incident 17: Dispute over child custody and maintenance after mutual divorce filing.
gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

fact_pattern: Incident 18: Employee was terminated without 1-month notice after refusing personal demands of the manager.

precedents.case_name: Vishaka v State of Rajasthan (1997 SC)
precedents.rationale: Workplace harassment protection.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

fact_pattern: Incident 19: The municipal corporation demolished the petitioner's pavement dwelling without prior notice.
statutes_applicable: Article 21 - Right to Life and Livelihood

precedents.case_name: Olga Tellis v Bombay Municipal Corporation (1985 SC)
precedents.rationale: Right to livelihood is part of Right to Life.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

precedents.case_name: State of Maharashtra v Madhurkar Narayan
precedents.rationale: Clear mens rea.

precedents.case_name: Satyabrata Ghose v Mugneeram Bangur (1954 SC)
precedents.rationale: Price rise is not force majeure.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

precedents.case_name: Vishaka v State of Rajasthan (1997 SC)
precedents.rationale: Workplace harassment protection.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

statutes_applicable: Article 21 - Right to Life and Livelihood

precedents.case_name: Olga Tellis v Bombay Municipal Corporation (1985 SC)
precedents.rationale: Right to livelihood is part of Right to Life.

precedents.rationale: Clear mens rea.

precedents.case_name: Satyabrata Ghose v Mugneeram Bangur (1954 SC)
precedents.rationale: Price rise is not force majeure.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

precedents.case_name: Vishaka v State of Rajasthan (1997 SC)
precedents.rationale: Workplace harassment protection.

statutes_applicable: Article 21 - Right to Life and Livelihood

precedents.case_name: Olga Tellis v Bombay Municipal Corporation (1985 SC)
precedents.rationale: Right to livelihood is part of Right to Life.

precedents.case_name: State of Maharashtra v Madhurkar Narayan
precedents.rationale: Clear mens rea.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

precedents.case_name: Satyabrata Ghose v Mugneeram Bangur (1954 SC)
precedents.rationale: Price rise is not force majeure.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

precedents.case_name: Vishaka v State of Rajasthan (1997 SC)
precedents.rationale: Workplace harassment protection.

precedents.rationale: Right to livelihood is part of Right to Life.

precedents.rationale: Clear mens rea.

precedents.case_name: Satyabrata Ghose v Mugneeram Bangur (1954 SC)
precedents.rationale: Price rise is not force majeure.

precedents.rationale: Right to livelihood is part of Right to Life.

precedents.case_name: Satyabrata Ghose v Mugneeram Bangur (1954 SC)
precedents.rationale: Price rise is not force majeure.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

precedents.case_name: Vishaka v State of Rajasthan (1997 SC)
precedents.rationale: Workplace harassment protection.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

statutes_applicable: Article 21 - Right to Life and Livelihood

precedents.case_name: Olga Tellis v Bombay Municipal Corporation (1985 SC)
precedents.rationale: Right to livelihood is part of Right to Life.

gold_reasoning: Standard IRAC application of the specified statute.

fact_pattern: A shopkeeper was caught on CCTV stealing electricity by tampering with the meter. The electricity board filed a complaint. The shopkeeper claims the meter was faulty.
applicable_statutes: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 135 - Theft of electricity
Electricity Act 2003, Section 135 - Theft of electricity

precedents.case_name: State v Rajesh Kumar (2024)
precedents.rationale: Electricity theft is cognizable. Matter forwarded to magistrate.

gold_label_reasoning: Criminal matter under BNS 2023. AI cannot adjudicate. Case forwarded to human judge for trial.

fact_pattern: Two neighbors had an altercation over a parking space. Neighbor A slapped Neighbor B in front of witnesses. Neighbor B filed an FIR under BNS provisions for simple assault.
applicable_statutes: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 115 - Voluntarily causing hurt
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 74 - Assault

precedents.case_name: Mohan v State (2025)
precedents.rationale: Simple assault is compoundable. Court may direct mediation first.

gold_label_reasoning: Criminal matter under BNS 2023. AI cannot adjudicate. Case forwarded to human judge for trial.

fact_pattern: A street vendor was arrested for selling counterfeit branded goods worth Rs. 50,000. The vendor claims he did not know the goods were fake and bought them from a wholesale market.
applicable_statutes: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 318 - Cheating
Trade Marks Act 1999, Section 103 - Penalty for selling goods with false trade marks

precedents.case_name: State v Counterfeit Seller (2024)
precedents.rationale: Selling counterfeit goods is punishable. Knowledge of counterfeiting is a question of fact for trial.

gold_label_reasoning: Criminal matter under BNS 2023. AI cannot adjudicate. Case forwarded to human judge for trial.

fact_pattern: A delivery driver was caught driving under the influence of alcohol after causing a minor fender-bender. No injuries occurred. The driver's blood alcohol level was 0.08%, just above the legal limit.
applicable_statutes: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 125 - Act endangering life or personal safety
Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Section 185 - Driving by a drunken person

precedents.case_name: State v Drunk Driver (2025)
precedents.rationale: DUI is a criminal offense. License suspension and fine to be determined by magistrate.

gold_label_reasoning: Criminal matter under BNS 2023. AI cannot adjudicate. Case forwarded to human judge for trial.

fact_pattern: A college student posted defamatory content about a professor on social media accusing them of taking bribes. The professor filed a complaint. The student claims it was a whistleblower post based on facts.
applicable_statutes: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 356 - Defamation
Information Technology Act 2000, Section 66A - Offensive messages (struck down but often cited)

precedents.case_name: Shreya Singhal v Union of India (2015)
precedents.rationale: Free speech must be balanced against defamation rights. Truth is a defense under BNS Section 356.

gold_label_reasoning: Criminal matter under BNS 2023. AI cannot adjudicate. Case forwarded to human judge for trial.

fact_pattern: A construction company failed to put warning signs around an open trench on a public road. A cyclist fell into the trench at night and broke his leg. The company claims the cyclist was riding without lights.
applicable_statutes: Law of Torts - Negligence and Duty of Care
Indian Penal Code Section 337 - Causing hurt by act endangering life

precedents.case_name: Municipal Corporation of Delhi v Subhagwanti (1966)
precedents.rationale: Entity creating hazard on public road must take adequate precautions. Failure to warn is negligence.

gold_label_reasoning: Construction company had primary duty to warn. Cyclist's lack of lights is contributory but does not absolve the company.

fact_pattern: A gym trainer pushed a client to do excessive weight training despite the client expressing discomfort. The client suffered a herniated disc. The gym's waiver form was signed by the client.
applicable_statutes: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2(42) - Deficiency in service
Law of Torts - Professional Negligence

precedents.case_name: Bolam v Friern Hospital (1957)
precedents.rationale: Professional must act within standards of a reasonable practitioner. Ignoring client distress is negligence.

gold_label_reasoning: Waiver does not cover negligence. Trainer ignored expressed distress, breaching duty of care.

fact_pattern: A dog owner's pet bit a child playing in a public park. The dog was not on a leash. The owner claims the child provoked the dog by throwing stones.
applicable_statutes: Law of Torts - Strict Liability for Animals
Municipal bylaws - Leash requirements in public spaces

precedents.case_name: Rylands v Fletcher (1868)
precedents.rationale: Keeper of a dangerous thing is strictly liable for harm it causes if it escapes control.

gold_label_reasoning: Dog owner strictly liable for unleashed dog. Provocation by child, if proven, may reduce damages but not liability.

fact_pattern: An e-commerce platform delivered a defective power bank that caught fire and damaged furniture worth Rs. 2 lakhs. The platform claims it is only a marketplace and the seller is liable.
applicable_statutes: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2(47) - Product liability
E-Commerce Rules 2020 - Marketplace duties

precedents.case_name: Amazon Seller Services v Consumer (2023)
precedents.rationale: E-commerce platforms share liability if they fail quality checks on listed products.

gold_label_reasoning: Platform failed quality verification duty. Joint liability with seller under Consumer Protection Act.

fact_pattern: A software company agreed to deliver a mobile app to a client in 60 days for Rs. 8 lakhs. After 90 days, only 40% was complete. The client terminated the contract and demanded a full refund of the Rs. 4 lakh advance.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 55 - Effect of failure to perform at fixed time
Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73 - Compensation for breach

precedents.case_name: Citrix Systems v Infosys (2017)
precedents.rationale: Service provider who fails to deliver on time must refund advance and pay damages.

gold_label_reasoning: Software company breached time-bound contract. Client entitled to full refund of advance plus damages.

fact_pattern: A farmer agreed to sell 1000 kg of rice at Rs. 30/kg to a buyer. Before delivery, market price rose to Rs. 45/kg. The farmer refused to deliver claiming the contract was unfair.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73 - Compensation for breach
Sale of Goods Act 1930, Section 56 - Buyer's right to damages

precedents.case_name: Krell v Henry (1903)
precedents.rationale: Price fluctuation alone does not frustrate a contract. Seller must honor agreed terms.

gold_label_reasoning: Price rise does not constitute frustration. Farmer must deliver at agreed price or pay difference as damages.

fact_pattern: A tuition teacher agreed to prepare a student for competitive exams over 6 months for Rs. 1.5 lakhs. After 2 months, the teacher stopped classes without notice. The student failed the exam.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 39 - Effect of refusal of party to perform wholly
Consumer Protection Act 2019 - Deficiency in service

precedents.case_name: Lucknow Development Authority v MK Gupta (1994)
precedents.rationale: Service provider who abandons contract midway is liable for refund and consequential damages.

gold_label_reasoning: Teacher breached contract by stopping classes. Student entitled to proportionate refund and damages.

fact_pattern: A government employee was transferred to a remote posting as 'punishment' after filing a corruption complaint against a senior officer. The employee claims violation of service rules and malicious intent.
applicable_statutes: Central Civil Services Rules - Transfer provisions
Whistle Blowers Protection Act 2014
Constitution of India, Article 311 - Dismissal, removal or reduction in rank

precedents.case_name: Union of India v SL Abbas (1993)
precedents.rationale: Transfer is an incident of service but cannot be used as punishment. Malice must be proven.

gold_label_reasoning: Timing of transfer suggests retaliation. Whistle blower entitled to protection. Transfer order quashed.

fact_pattern: A municipality denied building permission for a residential house citing 'environmental concerns' near a river bank. The applicant shows neighboring plots received permission. The municipality cannot produce the environmental study.
applicable_statutes: Constitution of India, Article 14 - Right to Equality
Environment Protection Act 1986 - Environmental clearance

precedents.case_name: Vellore Citizens Welfare Forum v Union of India (1996)
precedents.rationale: Environmental protection is paramount but must be applied consistently and with scientific basis.

gold_label_reasoning: Discriminatory denial without environmental study violates Article 14. Permission must be granted or consistent policy applied.

fact_pattern: A student with a disability was denied admission to a government college despite qualifying marks, citing 'lack of infrastructure for disabled students.' The student invokes the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act.
applicable_statutes: Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016, Section 32 - Reservation in higher education
Constitution of India, Article 21 - Right to Life and Education

precedents.case_name: Disabled Rights Group v Union of India (2018)
precedents.rationale: Educational institutions cannot deny admission on grounds of disability. Reasonable accommodation is mandatory.

gold_label_reasoning: Denial violates RPwD Act 2016. College must provide reasonable accommodation and grant admission.

fact_pattern: A factory discharged untreated waste into a river causing skin diseases in a nearby village. The factory claims it has a pollution control board certificate. Villagers filed a public interest litigation.
applicable_statutes: Environment Protection Act 1986 - Pollution control
Constitution of India, Article 21 - Right to clean environment

precedents.case_name: MC Mehta v Union of India (1987)
precedents.rationale: Absolute liability for hazardous activities. No defense of due diligence available.

gold_label_reasoning: Factory absolutely liable under MC Mehta principle. Expired PCB certificate confirms non-compliance.

fact_pattern: A private hospital refused emergency treatment to an accident victim demanding advance payment. The victim died. The family claims medical negligence and violation of right to life.
applicable_statutes: Consumer Protection Act 2019 - Medical negligence
Constitution of India, Article 21 - Right to Life

precedents.case_name: Parmanand Katara v Union of India (1989)
precedents.rationale: Every doctor and hospital has duty to provide emergency medical aid. Refusal is criminal negligence.

gold_label_reasoning: Hospital violated constitutional duty to provide emergency care. Liable for negligence causing death.

fact_pattern: An online retailer accepted an order for a laptop at Rs. 25,000 (displayed price). After payment, the retailer cancelled claiming it was a 'pricing error' and the actual price was Rs. 75,000.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 10 - What agreements are contracts
Consumer Protection Act 2019 - Unfair trade practices

precedents.case_name: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co (1893)
precedents.rationale: Offer accepted and paid for creates binding contract. Unilateral cancellation is breach.

gold_label_reasoning: Accepted order with payment constitutes valid contract. Seller cannot unilaterally cancel citing pricing error.

fact_pattern: A landlord refused to return a security deposit of Rs. 1 lakh claiming damages to the flat. The tenant has photos showing the flat was in good condition at the time of vacating. The lease agreement states deposit is refundable.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73 - Compensation for breach
Transfer of Property Act 1882, Section 108 - Rights and liabilities of lessor and lessee

precedents.case_name: Tenant Rights Association v Delhi (2019)
precedents.rationale: Security deposit must be returned if property is in reasonable condition. Landlord must prove actual damage.

gold_label_reasoning: Photographic evidence supports good condition. Landlord must return security deposit per lease terms.

fact_pattern: A wife seeks partition of a house jointly owned with her husband after divorce. The husband argues the house was bought with his earnings only. The wife contributed to household expenses enabling the purchase.
applicable_statutes: Hindu Marriage Act 1955, Section 27 - Disposal of property
Hindu Succession Act 1956 - Women's property rights

precedents.case_name: Pratibha Rani v Suraj Kumar (1985)
precedents.rationale: Wife's indirect contribution to household enables property acquisition, entitling her to a share.

gold_label_reasoning: Wife's contribution to household indirectly enabled purchase. Entitled to equitable share, not necessarily 50%.

fact_pattern: An NRI claims inherited agricultural land in Punjab. The state government argues NRIs cannot hold agricultural land under Punjab Land Reforms Act. The NRI inherited the land through succession, not purchase.
applicable_statutes: Punjab Land Reforms Act 1972 - Ceiling on land holdings
Hindu Succession Act 1956 - Succession rights

precedents.case_name: Bhag Singh v State of Punjab (2004)
precedents.rationale: Inheritance of agricultural land is a constitutional right distinct from purchase restrictions.

gold_label_reasoning: Inheritance rights cannot be curtailed by land reform acts. NRI entitled to inherited agricultural land.

fact_pattern: A temple trust claims ownership of land occupied by a family for 50 years. The family has no title deed but has been paying property tax. The trust produces a 100-year-old endowment deed.
applicable_statutes: Limitation Act 1963, Section 65 - Adverse possession
Hindu Religious Endowments Act - Trust property protection

precedents.case_name: Karnataka Board of Wakf v Govt of India (2004)
precedents.rationale: Religious endowment property cannot be claimed by adverse possession in many states.

gold_label_reasoning: Trust property is protected from adverse possession. Family must vacate but may claim compensation for improvements.

fact_pattern: A domestic worker is accused of stealing gold jewelry worth Rs. 3 lakhs from her employer. The worker claims the employer gifted the items. No written record of the alleged gift exists. CCTV shows the worker leaving with a bag.
applicable_statutes: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 303 - Theft
Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023, Section 317 - Criminal breach of trust

precedents.case_name: State v Domestic Worker (2024)
precedents.rationale: Theft vs gift dispute is a question of fact requiring trial. CCTV alone insufficient without corroboration.

gold_label_reasoning: Criminal matter under BNS 2023. AI cannot adjudicate. Case forwarded to human judge for trial.

fact_pattern: A school failed to supervise students during recess. A 10-year-old child fell from a broken railing and fractured his arm. The school claims the railing was damaged the same morning and they had not noticed.
applicable_statutes: Law of Torts - Duty of care towards children
Consumer Protection Act 2019 - Deficiency in service

precedents.case_name: Spring Meadows Hospital v Harjol Ahluwalia (1998)
precedents.rationale: Institution in charge of minor has heightened duty of care. Infrastructure must be maintained.

gold_label_reasoning: School has heightened duty of care for minor children. Failure to maintain safe premises is negligence.

fact_pattern: A ride-sharing driver took a longer route deliberately to inflate the fare. The passenger complained but was charged Rs. 800 instead of the estimated Rs. 350. The app shows the detour clearly.
applicable_statutes: Consumer Protection Act 2019 - Unfair trade practices
Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 17 - Fraud

precedents.case_name: Uber India v Consumer Forum (2022)
precedents.rationale: Platform and driver jointly liable for deliberate fare inflation through route manipulation.

gold_label_reasoning: Deliberate detour constitutes unfair trade practice. Driver and platform jointly liable for refund plus compensation.

fact_pattern: A catering company was hired for a corporate event for Rs. 3 lakhs. The food served caused food poisoning to 30 attendees. The catering company claims the venue's storage was improper.
applicable_statutes: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73 - Compensation for breach
Food Safety and Standards Act 2006 - Food safety

precedents.case_name: Ashby v White (1703)
precedents.rationale: Where there is a right, there is a remedy. Breach of implied warranty of fitness.

gold_label_reasoning: Caterer has implied duty to provide safe food. Venue storage was adequate. Caterer fully liable.

fact_pattern: A passport renewal application was rejected without reason after being pending for 8 months. The applicant has no criminal record and all documents are in order. The passport office cites 'verification pending' without specifying what verification.
applicable_statutes: Passports Act 1967, Section 5 - Application for passport
Constitution of India, Article 21 - Right to travel

precedents.case_name: Maneka Gandhi v Union of India (1978)
precedents.rationale: Right to travel is a fundamental right under Article 21. Passport cannot be withheld without specific reasons.

gold_label_reasoning: Withholding passport without specific reason violates Article 21. Passport office directed to issue within 30 days.
